title: `hostPort` port used by `podman kube play` can be reused by other applications
body:
### Issue Description

After creating a Pod with a `hostPort` defined in it, I noticed that any other applications might be able to listen to the same port again (thus hijacking the port). 
I've checked with Podman v3 (3.4.4), and it behaves as expected to me, but not Podman v4 (4.4.2).
Maybe I've missed it but I didn't see any mention of that in the release notes, and was wondering if that was intentional. 

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create the pod and expose its port via `hostPort`
``` shell
cat << EOF | podman kube play -               
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: python-web
spec:
  containers:
  - name: python-web-container
    image: quay.io/redhatworkshops/simple-python-web:latest
    command: [ '/usr/bin/python3', '-m', 'http.server', '9000', '--bind', '0.0.0.0']
    ports:
    - containerPort: 9000
      hostPort: 8998
EOF
```

2. Check that connecting to the `hostPort` works correctly:
```shell
curl http://localhost:8998
```

3. Now try to listen to the same `hostPort` from an external application, e.g. with `nc`:
``` shell
# I would expect this to fail
nc -v -l 127.0.0.1 8998
```


### Describe the results you received

The `nc` command above succeeds to listen to the same `hostPort`:

```shell
$ nc -v -l 127.0.0.1 8998
Ncat: Version 7.93 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
Ncat: Listening on 127.0.0.1:8998
```

### Describe the results you expected

I would expect the `nc` command would not succeed because it is trying to listen to a port that is already in use.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Podman v3 (tested on 3.4.4) did not behave this way. If I run the same commands with Podman v3, the `nc` command returns `nc: Address already in use`.

comments.body:
Is your container still up and running when you check with nc?
What does `ss -tlpn` say? If you run rootless the rootlessport process should have bound the port.

@Luap99 Thanks for the prompt response.

> Is your container still up and running when you check with nc?

Yes, the container was still up and running when I checked with `nc`:

```
$ podman pod ls                                 
POD ID        NAME        STATUS      CREATED         INFRA ID      # OF CONTAINERS
3fba66c89a00  python-web  Running     37 minutes ago  a48be47665b9  2

$ podman container ls                                                
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                                             COMMAND     CREATED         STATUS         PORTS                   NAMES
a48be47665b9  localhost/podman-pause:4.4.2-1677669779                       37 minutes ago  Up 37 minutes  0.0.0.0:8998->9000/tcp  3fba66c89a00-infra
788f1652c4c1  quay.io/redhatworkshops/simple-python-web:latest              37 minutes ago  Up 37 minutes  0.0.0.0:8998->9000/tcp  python-web-python-web-container

$ nc -v -l 127.0.0.1 8998
Ncat: Version 7.93 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
Ncat: Listening on 127.0.0.1:8998
```

> What does `ss -tlpn` say? If you run rootless the rootlessport process should have bound the port.

Here is the output of `ss -tlpn`:

```
$ ss -tlpn
State               Recv-Q              Send-Q                                Local Address:Port                            Peer Address:Port             Process                                                   
LISTEN              0                   4096                                  127.0.0.53%lo:53                                   0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   128                                       127.0.0.1:631                                  0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   4096                                     127.0.0.54:53                                   0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   4096                                        0.0.0.0:5000                                 0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   4096                                        0.0.0.0:5355                                 0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   4096                                        0.0.0.0:6443                                 0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   4096                                        0.0.0.0:80                                   0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   128                                         0.0.0.0:22                                   0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   4096                                        0.0.0.0:443                                  0.0.0.0:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   128                                           [::1]:631                                     [::]:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   4096                             [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:6942                                       *:*                 users:(("java",pid=149232,fd=13))                        
LISTEN              0                   4096                                              *:8998                                       *:*                 users:(("rootlessport",pid=1306306,fd=11))               
LISTEN              0                   4096                             [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:63342                                      *:*                 users:(("java",pid=149232,fd=67))                        
LISTEN              0                   4096                                           [::]:5000                                    [::]:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   50                               [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:52829                                      *:*                 users:(("jetbrains-toolb",pid=141354,fd=61))             
LISTEN              0                   4096                                           [::]:5355                                    [::]:*                                                                          
LISTEN              0                   128                                            [::]:22                                      [::]:*                                                                          
```

What does ss say when nc is also listening?

> LISTEN              0                   4096                                              *:8998                                       *:*                 users:(("rootlessport",pid=1306306,fd=11))               

Port is bound by us as expected so it doesn't look like a problem with podman.
What kernel are you using? There was a bug in the 6.0 series where bind no longer failed with address in use.

> What does ss say when nc is also listening?
> 
Here is the output when both `rootlessport` and `nc` processes are running:
```
$ ss -tlpn | grep 8998
LISTEN 0      10              127.0.0.1:8998       0.0.0.0:*    users:(("nc",pid=1680488,fd=3))             
LISTEN 0      4096                    *:8998             *:*    users:(("rootlessport",pid=1306306,fd=11))                                                                      
```

> > LISTEN              0                   4096                                              *:8998                                       _:_                 users:(("rootlessport",pid=1306306,fd=11))
> 
> Port is bound by us as expected so it doesn't look like a problem with podman. What kernel are you using? There was a bug in the 6.0 series where bind no longer failed with the address in use.

Yes, I initially suspected a similar kernel issue, which I had also run into it a few weeks ago. But now that I am using a more recent kernel (`6.1.14-200.fc37.x86_64)`, I didn't run into this with other programs. And this does not seem to affect Podman v3 (3.4.4) on the same kernel.

Maybe I'm missing something, but if I try to simulate this issue with `nc`, it works as expected, hence my confusion:
```shell
# terminal 1
$ nc -v -l 0.0.0.0 1234 
Ncat: Version 7.93 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
Ncat: Listening on 0.0.0.0:1234

# terminal 2
$ nc -v -l 127.0.0.1 1234 
Ncat: Version 7.93 ( https://nmap.org/ncat )
Ncat: bind to 127.0.0.1:1234: Address already in use. QUITTING.
```

The consequence is that this issue prevents the tool I am working on from detecting if the `hostPort`  opened by Podman is free or not (it does so by trying to listen on `127.0.0.1:$port`).

I don't recall any difference between 3.4 and 4.4 that would cause it. Could you show the ss output with 3.4?

This still looks like a kernel bug, per [ipv6(7)](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/ipv6.7.html):
>  IPv4 connections can be handled with the v6 API by using the
       v4-mapped-on-v6 address type; thus a program needs to support
       only this API type to support both protocols.  This is handled
       transparently by the address handling functions in the C library.

>  IPv4 and IPv6 share the local port space.  When you get an IPv4
       connection or packet to an IPv6 socket, its source address will
       be mapped to v6 and it will be mapped to v6.

So only binding the ipv6 port will also bind the ipv4 port (unless IPV6_V6ONLY was set). You can see this working because curl 127.0.0.1:8998 works but as soon as the nc process starts listening the curl call gets routed to the nc process instead.

Also if I inverse the order, so first bind 127.0.0.1 then run podman it correctly fails with address in use.

Ok, I bisect this to kernel commit `5456262d2baa net: Fix incorrect address comparison when searching for a bind2 bucket`
I will report this on the kernel mailing list as regression.

Thanks for narrowing down the issue to the kernel.
I noticed that my previous test of Podman 3.4 was actually running on an older 5.19 kernel. I just tried again on a more recent kernel (6.2.6), and Podman v3 has the same behavior.

I also tried running your code above on both kernels (6.1.15 and 6.2.6), and indeed, the same port is bound twice.

> Ok, I bisect this to kernel commit `5456262d2baa net: Fix incorrect address comparison when searching for a bind2 bucket` I will report this on the kernel mailing list as regression.

Thanks. I'll keep an eye out then for a newer kernel.

Meanwhile, I think a workaround for us could be to explicitly set `hostIP` in the Pod spec to `127.0.0.1` for now.

Thank you very much for your support on this. I guess we can close this issue now.

You could also check for the port with `nc -v -l 0.0.0.0 8998`, this fails correctly. Only for a specific ipv4 it does not fail.

Kernel BUG: https://lore.kernel.org/stable/e21bf153-80b0-9ec0-15ba-e04a4ad42c34@redhat.com/T/
PATCH: https://lore.kernel.org/netdev/20230312031904.4674-3-kuniyu@amazon.com/T/
